items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: What is the difference between act and rule utilitarianism?
items.correct: Act utilitarianism judges each individual action by its consequences; rule utilitarianism follows rules that, if generally adopted, would produce the most good — sacrificing case-by-case calculation for predictability and fairness.
items.misconception: Act and rule utilitarianism are basically the same approach.
items.random_wrong: Act utilitarianism is about action movies and rule utilitarianism is about laws.
items.why: The distinction matters for famous counterexamples (e.g. punishing the innocent). Act utilitarianism might endorse it if outcomes are better; rule utilitarianism rejects such practices because the rule would be harmful overall.

items.q: Kant's three postulates of practical reason are:
items.why: Correct answer: God, freedom, immortality. “God, virtue, happiness” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Quantitative. “Qualitative” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many postulates of practical reason does Kant identify?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
